content.text: In Russian, how do you say 'Hello - Formal'? {{c1::Здравствуйте (Zdrávstvuyte)}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Hello - Formal' en Russian? Здравствуйте (Zdrávstvuyte)

content.text: In Russian, how do you say 'Hi - Informal'? {{c1::Привет (Privét)}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Hi - Informal' en Russian? Привет (Privét)

content.text: In Russian, how do you say 'Goodbye'? {{c1::До свидания (Do svidániya)}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Goodbye' en Russian? До свидания (Do svidániya)

content.text: In Russian, how do you say 'Bye - Informal'? {{c1::Пока (Poká)}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Bye - Informal' en Russian? Пока (Poká)

content.text: In Russian, how do you say 'Good morning'? {{c1::Доброе утро (Dóbroye útro)}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Good morning' en Russian? Доброе утро (Dóbroye útro)
content.usage: Term: Доброе утро (Dóbroye útro) | Meaning: Good morning

content.text: In Russian, how do you say 'How are you?'? {{c1::Как дела? (Kak delá?)}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'How are you?' en Russian? Как дела? (Kak delá?)
content.usage: Term: Как дела? (Kak delá?) | Meaning: How are you?

content.text: In Russian, how do you say 'Good night'? {{c1::Спокойной ноchi (Spokóynoy nóchi)}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Good night' en Russian? Спокойной ноchi (Spokóynoy nóchi)
content.usage: Term: Спокойной ноchi (Spokóynoy nóchi) | Meaning: Good night

content.text: In Russian, the polite and formal greeting for 'Hello' (to strangers, elders, or superiors) is '{{c1::Здравствуйте}}' (Zdravstvuyte).
content.explanation: Contains a silent 'v' (в) in the middle. Pronounced: /zdrast-vuy-tye/.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Hola' formalmente en ruso? Здравствуйте (Zdravstvuyte).

content.text: In Russian, the informal greeting 'Hello / Hi' (for friends or peers) is '{{c1::Привет}}' (Privet).
content.explanation: A very friendly and casual greeting.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Hola' informalmente en ruso? Привет (Privet).
content.usage: Term: Привет | Dialogue: - Привет, Анна! - Привет, Иван!

content.text: In Russian, how do you say 'Good morning'? {{c1::Доброе утро}} (Dobroye utro)
content.explanation: Used from early morning until around noon.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Buenos días' en ruso? Доброе утро (Dobroye utro).

content.text: In Russian, how do you say 'Good afternoon'? {{c1::Добрый день}} (Dobryy den')
content.explanation: The most common daytime greeting from noon until evening.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Buenas tardes' en ruso? Добрый день (Dobryy den').

content.text: In Russian, how do you say 'Good evening'? {{c1::Добрый вечер}} (Dobryy vecher)
content.explanation: Greeting used after sunset or in the evening.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Buenas noches (saludo)' en ruso? Добрый вечер (Dobryy vecher).

content.text: In Russian, how do you say 'Goodbye' formally? {{c1::До свидания}} (Do svidaniya)
content.explanation: Literally: 'Until meeting'. The universal formal farewell.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Adiós' formalmente en ruso? До свидания (Do svidaniya).

content.text: In Russian, how do you say 'Bye' informally? {{c1::Пока}} (Poka)
content.explanation: Used with friends, family, and peers.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Adiós' informalmente en ruso? Пока (Poka).

content.text: In Russian, the versatile word for both 'Please' and 'You're welcome' is '{{c1::Пожалуйста}}' (Pozhaluysta).
content.explanation: Pronounced roughly: /pa-zha-luy-sta/. The first 'u' (у) and 'y' (й) are often blended in natural speech.
content.spanish: En ruso, la palabra 'Пожалуйста' (Pozhaluysta) significa tanto 'Por favor' como 'De nada'.
content.usage: Examples: Дайте, пожалуйста, воду. (Please give me water.) | - Спасибо! - Пожалуйста!

content.text: In Russian, how do you say 'Thank you'? {{c1::Спасибо}} (Spasibo)
content.explanation: The standard, most common way to show gratitude.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Gracias' en ruso? Спасибо (Spasibo).
content.usage: Term: Спасибо | Example: Спасибо за помощь. (Thank you for the help.)

content.text: In Russian, how do you ask someone their name formally? '{{c1::Как вас зовут?}}' (Kak vas zovut?) and the response is '{{c2::Меня зовут}} [Name]' (Menya zovut...).
content.explanation: Literally: 'How do they call you?' / 'They call me...'. 'вас' is the formal/plural 'you' in accusative.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice '¿Cómo se llama?' en ruso? Как вас зовут? y la respuesta es Меня зовут...
content.usage: Dialogue: - Как вас зовут? - Меня зовут Jesus.

content.text: In Russian, how do you say 'Nice to meet you'? {{c1::Очень приятно}} (Ochen' priyatno)
content.explanation: Literally: 'Very pleasant'. Short and standard phrase.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Mucho gusto' en ruso? Очень приятно (Ochen' priyatno).
content.usage: Dialogue: - Меня зовут Анна. - Очень приятно, я Иван.

content.text: In Russian, how do you ask 'How are things? / How are you?'? {{c1::Как дела?}} (Kak dela?)
content.explanation: The standard casual question. Response can be 'Хорошо' (Good), 'Нормально' (Normal), or 'Плохо' (Bad).
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice '¿Cómo estás / Cómo te va?' en ruso? Как дела? (Kak dela?).

content.text: In Russian, how do you say 'Good night' (when going to bed)? {{c1::Спокойной ночи}} (Spokoynoy nochi)
content.explanation: Literally: 'Of peaceful night'. Said to someone going to sleep.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Buenas noches (al ir a dormir)' en ruso? Спокойной ночи.

content.scenario: Russian A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In Russian, how do you say 'I am '? {{c1::Я ...}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'I am ' en Russian? Я ...

content.scenario: Russian A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In Russian, how do you say 'You are ... (informal)'? {{c1::Ты ...}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'You are ... (informal)' en Russian? Ты ...

content.scenario: Russian A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In Russian, how do you say 'He/She is '? {{c1::Он/Она ...}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'He/She is ' en Russian? Он/Она ...

content.scenario: Russian A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In Russian, how do you say 'We are '? {{c1::Мы ...}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'We are ' en Russian? Мы ...

content.scenario: Russian A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In Russian, how do you say 'They are '? {{c1::Они ...}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'They are ' en Russian? Они ...

content.scenario: Russian A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In Russian, how do you say 'Not'? {{c1::не (ne)}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Not' en Russian? не (ne)

content.text: In Russian, the subject pronouns for 'I' and 'you (singular informal)' are {{c1::я}} (ya) and {{c2::ты}} (ty).
content.explanation: Subject pronouns are used in front of verbs and nouns.
content.spanish: En ruso, los pronombres para 'yo' y 'tú' son я y ты.
content.usage: Examples: Я студент. (I am a student.) | Ты дома? (Are you at home?)

content.text: In Russian, the third-person singular pronouns for 'he', 'she', and 'it' are {{c1::он}} (on), {{c2::она}} (ona), and {{c3::оно}} (ono).
content.explanation: Russian nouns have three genders, and pronouns match the gender of the noun.
content.spanish: En ruso, los pronombres de tercera persona singular son он (él), она (ella) y оно (ello/neutro).
content.usage: Examples: Он врач. (He is a doctor.) | Она учитель. (She is a teacher.)

content.text: In Russian, the plural subject pronouns for 'we', 'you (formal/plural)', and 'they' are {{c1::мы}} (my), {{c2::вы}} (vy), and {{c3::они}} (oni).
content.explanation: 'вы' is capitalized as 'Вы' when writing to one person formally.
content.spanish: En ruso, los pronombres de sujeto plurales son мы (nosotros), вы (vosotros/ustedes/formal) y они (ellos/ellas).
content.usage: Examples: Мы готовы. (We are ready.) | Они здесь. (They are here.)

content.text: In present tense Russian, the verb 'to be' (am / is / are) is {{c1::omitted / not used}}. Example: 'I am a doctor' is translated simply as '{{c2::Я врач}}'.
content.explanation: The present copula (есть) exists but is almost never used in simple identity statements. A dash is sometimes used in writing (e.g. Я — врач).
content.spanish: En el presente del ruso, el verbo 'ser/estar' se omite. 'Я врач' = Yo soy médico.
content.usage: Formula: Subject + Noun. Example: Я инженер. (I am an engineer.)

content.text: To negate a sentence in Russian, you place the particle '{{c1::не}}' (nye) directly before the word being negated. Example: I am not a student = {{c2::Я не студент}}.
content.explanation: Simple negation by placing 'не' before the noun, verb, or adjective.
content.spanish: Para negar en ruso, se coloca la partícula 'не' directamente antes de la palabra. 'Я не студент' = No soy estudiante.
content.usage: Example: Он не здесь. (He is not here.)

content.text: In Russian, the possessive pronouns for 'my' and 'your (informal)' in masculine form are {{c1::мой}} (moy) and {{c2::твой}} (tvoy).
content.explanation: Possessives must agree in gender with the noun they modify. (мой - masc, моя - fem, моё - neuter).
content.spanish: En ruso, los posesivos masculinos para 'mi' y 'tu' son мой y твой. Concuerdan en género con el sustantivo.

content.text: In Russian, the possessive pronouns for 'our' and 'your (formal/plural)' in masculine form are {{c1::наш}} (nash) and {{c2::ваш}} (vash).
content.explanation: They modify nouns and agree in gender (e.g. наш дом, наша страна).
content.spanish: En ruso, los posesivos masculinos para 'nuestro' y 'vuestro/su' son наш y ваш.

content.text: In Russian, the possessive pronouns for 'his', 'her', and 'their' are {{c1::его}} (yevo), {{c2::её}} (yeyo), and {{c3::их}} (ikh).
content.explanation: These possessive pronouns are invariable — they do not change according to the gender or number of the noun they modify.
content.spanish: En ruso, los posesivos invariables 'su' (de él), 'su' (de ella) y 'su' (de ellos) son его, её y их.
content.usage: Examples: его книга (his book) | её дом (her house) | их дети (their children)

content.text: In Russian, the pronoun 'me' in the phrase 'Menya zovut' (My name is) is '{{c1::меня}}' (menya), which is the accusative case of 'я'.
content.explanation: Literally means 'They call me...'.
content.spanish: El pronombre 'меня' (menya) en 'Menya zovut' es el acusativo de я (yo). Significa 'A mí me llaman'.

content.text: In Russian, the question word for 'Who' is '{{c1::Кто}}' (Kto) and for 'What' is '{{c2::Что}}' (pronounced 'shto').
content.explanation: Note that 'Что' is written with 'ч' but pronounced as 'ш' (sh).
content.spanish: En ruso, '¿Quién?' es 'Кто' y '¿Qué?' es 'Что' (se pronuncia 'shto').
content.usage: Examples: Кто это? (Who is this?) | Что это? (What is this?)

content.text: In Russian, the phrase used to say 'this is / that is / these are' pointing to things is '{{c1::это}}' (eto).
content.explanation: An invariable demonstrative pronoun used for identification.
content.spanish: En ruso, la palabra invariable usada para señalar y decir 'esto es / eso es' es 'это' (eto).
content.usage: Example: Это мой телефон. (This is my phone.) | Это наши друзья. (These are our friends.)

content.text: To state location in Russian, use 'где' (where) followed by the subject. Present verb is omitted. Example: Where is the hotel? = {{c1::Где гостиница?}}
content.explanation: No verb 'to be' is needed in the present tense for location either.
content.spanish: Para indicar ubicación en ruso, se usa 'где' seguido de la palabra. 'Где гостиница?' = ¿Dónde está el hotel?
content.usage: Dialogue: - Где метро? - Метро там. (- Where is the metro? - The metro is there.)

content.text: In Russian, the formal/respectful pronoun for 'you' is '{{c1::Вы}}' (with capital 'В' in writing) and the informal is '{{c2::ты}}'.
content.explanation: Always use 'Вы' with teachers, doctors, customers, and strangers.
content.spanish: El pronombre formal 'usted' en ruso es 'Вы' (con V mayúscula) y el informal 'tú' es 'ты'.
content.usage: Formal: Как Вы поживаете? (How are you? - formal)

content.text: In Russian, the pronoun 'you' in the formal question 'Kak vas zovut?' (What is your name?) is '{{c1::вас}}' (vas), which is the accusative case of 'вы'.
content.explanation: Literally: 'How do they call you?'
content.spanish: El pronombre 'вас' (vas) en 'Как вас зовут?' es el acusativo de вы.
